title: Application root and rendering

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Application root and rendering; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to Application root and rendering
sources.claim: A React root owns rendering for one DOM container and reconciles component output into that managed subtree.

sources.locator: React Versions; 19.2.7, June 2026; provisions applicable to Application root and rendering
sources.claim: A React root owns rendering for one DOM container and reconciles component output into that managed subtree.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A React root owns rendering for one DOM container and reconciles component output into that managed subtree.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: createRoot(container).render(<App />) gives React ownership of the app container without replacing unrelated page landmarks. Your goal is to create and explain a minimal React root and component tree. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: javascript project browser app; javascript runtime modules; javascript dom query update. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: A React root owns rendering for one DOM container and reconciles component output into that managed subtree. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Multiple roots do not share context automatically, hydration has stricter matching, and external DOM mutation can conflict. This draft therefore rejects: Calling render appends a fresh duplicate DOM tree on every state change.

includes: Mount one React root and distinguish rendering descriptions from DOM commit work.
Create and explain a minimal React root and component tree.
excludes: Multiple roots do not share context automatically, hydration has stricter matching, and external DOM mutation can conflict.

outcomes: Create and explain a minimal React root and component tree.

examples.title: Application root and rendering: concrete case
examples.situation: createRoot(container).render(<App />) gives React ownership of the app container without replacing unrelated page landmarks.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: createRoot(container).render(<App />) gives React ownership of the app container without replacing unrelated page landmarks.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: React reconciles a root's managed subtree rather than blindly appending a new tree each render.
examples.result: The result follows because React reconciles a root's managed subtree rather than blindly appending a new tree each render.

Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Calling render appends a fresh duplicate DOM tree on every state change.
